# 1\. Identifying & Defining {#1.-identifying-&-defining}

## 1.1 Problem Statement {#1.1-problem-statement}

There are few things that people typically encounter in their life that can strengthen their ability at long-term planning.

For many people, the only thing that requires long-term planning is financial planning, something that if done incorrectly can very negatively impact someone’s life. By providing a game that allows for people to regularly interact with the long-term consequences of their actions, it can improve their own ability to manage plans and control the outcomes of their life.

Software is the most effective way to create a deep strategy game for the player. By using systems that interact with each other in complex or deep ways, players are forced to strategically plan for how these systems will interact in the future. In other, more manual mediums, like board games, more complex systems require exponentially more effort when included, because players need to both manage the consequences of a system, and the direct outcomes and interactions of the system. This provides a clear benefit of software, because it allows for players to have a deep and interactive experience, whilst minimising difficulties and tedium.  
However, including other players provides a method of producing depth without relying on complex systems, and thus, on software. By allowing other players to play in the same game, competition forces players to act and interact with the systems in a more careful way, to avoid giving the others an advantage. The downside of this method, unfortunately, is that it is sometimes difficult to find a group in order to play a game, especially games that have to be played in person, like board games, still providing a clear benefit to software, due to its ability to be used at any time.

## 1.2 Project Purpose & Boundaries {#1.2-project-purpose-&-boundaries}

This project is attempting to provide a fun and enjoyable strategy game that uses features such as tile placement, in order to cause the user to consider and plan for the long-term consequences of their actions.

## 1.3 Stakeholder Requirements {#1.3-stakeholder-requirements}

The stakeholders most concerned with the outcome of this project would be the players of the game.

The requirements most important to the stakeholders involve accessibility and the ease of understanding the product. In order to provide an experience that is productive, users need to be able to interact in a meaningful way as easily as possible, so it is a requirement that the systems in place must be easy to make a simple judgement on, but difficult to make a complete judgement on.

## 1.4 Function Requirements {#1.4-function-requirements}

The system must:

* Have a working grid system that stores tile positions and adjacencies  
* Have a functional scoring system based on adjacencies that guides users into making positive decisions  
* Have a functional tile placement mechanic that allows users to interact with the tile systems, and provide difficulty in the form of random chance

## 1.5 Non-Functional Requirements {#1.5-non-functional-requirements}

The system should:

* Authenticate the user account  
* Store user scores under their account  
* Authorise users to access scores   
* Encrypt user data  
* Score and adjacency mechanics should take less than 2 seconds to complete from the user placing an input  
* Highly accessible menus with some customisation 

## 1.6 Constraints {#1.6-constraints}

There are a few notable constraints with this project, the most significant being the time constraint, and technical knowledge.

* Needs to be completed with Pygame, due to lack of technical ability in other tools  
* Needs to be completed by the end of term 2

## 1.7 Requirements Analysis & Prioritisation {#1.7-requirements-analysis-&-prioritisation}

The highest priority requirements are: the scoring system, in order to give a measure of improvement for the player, the grid system, in order to allow for the scoring system to function, and accessibility features, so that many players can have an enjoyable and productive experience. Due to the decision to use Pygame, it may be difficult to properly optimise the code to an acceptable degree. 

These requirements effectively address the problem of improving long-term planning ability because they give a suitable environment for improvement for the player. The scoring system gives a mark of improvement for the player as they are playing, the accessibility allows as many users as possible to play, and the storage of scores, to give the player a way to keep track of their improvement as a player.

# 2\. Researching & Planning {#2.-researching-&-planning}

## 2.1 Development Methodology {#2.1-development-methodology}

Waterfall will be used in this project, which focuses on forward planning to create applications, and performs tasks one after another. However, some minor alterations may be made in order to receive feedback during the development process, to make sure that the project is sticking to the guidelines.

Waterfall is being used for this project because it means that effective planning can be done prior to the development of the project. This project has a large scope over a large timescale, so it is likely that scope-creep can occur, and the overall changing of the project to make it not fit guidelines. Waterfall prevents these issues by requiring the planning of the project as a whole before development begins.   
However, waterfall struggles to incorporate feedback into the development process, something that needs to be accounted for during production. While this can have a huge impact on the direction of the project, this can be reduced with some use of feedback for individual systems, rather than the application as a whole. 

## 2.2 Tools & Technologies {#2.2-tools-&-technologies}

* **Language:** Python 3.13  
* **IDE:** VS Code  
* **Libraries:** pygame (graphics, user input)

## 2.3 GANTT Chart {#2.3-gantt-chart}

## ![][image1]

## 2.4 Communication Plan {#2.4-communication-plan}

Every time a significant addition to the system is completed, peer feedback will be acquired and recorded, and suggestions that can be completed in a reasonable amount of time will be incorporated into the program.  
To acquire the peer feedback, people will be asked to interact with and use the system, and written feedback will be collected on both the individual changes made, and the system as a whole.  
The written feedback will contain; things that were important qualities, things that should be changed, and overall suggestions on project direction.

## 2.5 Resource Allocation Justification {#2.5-resource-allocation-justification}

The overall resources required for this project are most simply time, and input. The amount of time that is allowed is fairly clear, as the project is meant to be completed by the end of the term, so certain constraints have been made in order to ensure that the project is completable under the necessary timeframe, most notably not using a new language that may be more effective, if it would take longer to learn. The other resource, input, indicates feedback given on the nature of the project from peers, in order to ensure that the final result is as high quality as possible. Input will be accessed at several significant points throughout the creation process.

# 3\. System Design {#3.-system-design}

## 3.1 Context Diagram {#3.1-context-diagram}

![][image2]

## 3.2 Data Flow Diagram {#3.2-data-flow-diagram}

![][image3]

## 3.3 Structure Chart {#3.3-structure-chart}

![][image4]

## 3.4 IPO Chart {#3.4-ipo-chart}

| Input | Process | Output |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Username and password | Validates username and password, stores credentials, authenticates user | Login result, access to scores, storage of scores |
| Mouse movement and clicks | Initialises game, begins login process, enters settings | Open game, login page, settings page |
| Mouse movements and clicks | Place tile, select tile | Board |

## 3.5 Data Dictionary {#3.5-data-dictionary}

| Name | Data Type | Size/Format | Description | Example | Constraints |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Score | int | x | Integer score of the player during the game | 126 | Must be an integer above zero |
| board\_tiles | list | \[\[...\]...\[...\]\] | List of the tile in the board in a 2d list | \[\[forest\], \[office\]\] | Must contain only lists of tile objects  |
| stack\_tiles | list | \[...\] | List of the tiles in the draw stack | \[field, house\] | Must contain only tile objects |
| tile\_type | str | t…t | String used to calculate adjacency bonuses | forest | No constraints |
| tile\_score | int | x | The amount of score that a tile will contribute to the final score | 4 | Must be an integer |
| Login details | list | \[..., …\] | The details the player used to login | \[oliver, password1\] | Must contain two strings |

## 3.6 UML Class Diagram {#3.6-uml-class-diagram}

![][image5]

# 4\. Producing and Implementing {#4.-producing-and-implementing}

## 4.1 Development Process {#4.1-development-process}

This program was created through primarily waterfall-style procedure. The board was created to be fully functional before moving onto the menu and login systems, which were then fully functional before further effort in design and graphics were put in.

This system uses several techniques from OOP, most notably in the Board class, which uses a collection of Tiles that describe some of their own information to offload code from the Board. The scoring system used in the board uses a large amount of error handling, as it is the primary way in which the board knows how to display and handle how tiles behave, in addition to other systems that use error handling. There is also simple validation in the login system, however there is little that relies on it.

## 4.2 Key Feature Developed {#4.2-key-feature-developed}

Inside of this system is a game that involves the matching of tiles using adjacency detection, and a login and high score storage system. Tiles can be placed on the board using a ‘hand’ mechanic where the player has a number of options to choose from, and the login system uses simple file loading and writing to save usernames, passwords, and highscores.

The use of tiles and a hand mechanic allows for meaningful strategic choices to be made in reference to the board state in order to generate more score. The login and storing system is designed to allow for improvement over time and provide further incentive to continue playing the game.

### 4.2.1 Back-End Engineering Contribution {#4.2.1-back-end-engineering-contribution}

Back-end engineering helped with the overall success of this application by improving how the user interacts with the system. The back-end components are the storage and application of the board, and the storage of login details and high scores. There is very little data processed in the login system, apart from username, password, and highscore, and they are stored simply together with the username and password being displayed on the home screen. The system doesn’t directly inform the user of any issues, though provides obvious enough cues that their login was unsuccessful, specifically the lack of change in the login message on the menu screen. 

## 4.3 Screenshots of Interface![][image6]![][image7]![][image8] {#4.3-screenshots-of-interface}

## 

# 5\. Testing and Evaluation {#5.-testing-and-evaluation}

## 5.1 Testing Methods Used {#5.1-testing-methods-used}

There were a few methods undertaken to ensure that the code functioned properly. During the development process, unit testing was done to ensure that each component was working properly before it interacted with other parts, and some user testing was done to ensure that the code was usable by others.   
The results of testing allowed for the program to be properly engineered to improve reliability. During development, many errors were found that resulted in the collapse of the program, which were all resolved completely to ensure that the program remains in every instance. 

## 5.2 Test Cases and Results {#5.2-test-cases-and-results}

| Test ID | Description | Expected Result | Actual Result | Pass/Fail |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| TC01 | Correct login | Correct username and score displayed | Correct username and score displayed | Pass  |
| TC02 | Incorrect username | No change in display | No change in display | Pass |
| TC03 | Incorrect password | No change in display | No change in display | Pass |
| TC04 | Strange characters in sign up | Create account with character | Create account with character\* | Pass |
| TC05 | Backslash in login | Create account with character | Create account with backslash backslashed\*\* | Pass |
| TC06 | Tiles placed in regular locations | Tiles generated in correct locations | Tiles generated in correct locations | Pass |
| TC07 | Tiles placed in strange places | No tile placed | No tile placed | Pass |

\* it doesn’t matter, but TAB and ENTER look really funny in the login\_details.txt  
\*\* so it doesn’t read as a special character

## 5.3 Evaluation Against Requirements {#5.3-evaluation-against-requirements}

This program fulfills many of the original requirements placed upon it. It succeeds in completing every gameplay mechanic, involving the board, the scoring and the tile placement, while fulfilling most of the non-functional requirements, like authentication and authorisation, score storage, some encryption, and function speed. However, very little has been implemented in the form of accessibility, one of the requirements. Overall, the program fulfills the requirements originally intended, though falls short of the full intended vision. 

This program has not been designed with the intent of fulfilling legislative requirements, though some effort has been undertaken to improve user privacy. User data is stored in a text file with no encryption done apart from the password, which was hashed, an obvious safety concern, however, due to the lack of use of user data, and the exclusive existence of user data locally, little thought was necessary to be taken place on these issues. 

## 5.4 Improvements and Future Development {#5.4-improvements-and-future-development}

This project was intended to have very simple tile mechanics with exclusively numerical bonuses offered, and a login system designed to just store scores

In the future this project would have additional tile mechanics added, most likely tiles that grant you more tiles to place, and tiles that change other tiles. Another mechanic that would likely be added is further use of the login system, most importantly the implementation of encryption, and the addition of other game modes that provide different challenges for the user.

## 5.5 Evaluation of Social, Ethical and Communication Issues {#5.5-evaluation-of-social,-ethical-and-communication-issues}

There is little in the domain of social, ethical and communication issues present in this project, as there is little cross-user interactions, reducing the concern. The system offers no method of communication between players, drastically decreasing the ability for these issues to arise, and because very little user data is stored, there is very little chance for a loss of privacy for the user, a significant ethical issue on other platforms. It is clear this project has none of these issues

# 6\. Feedback, Security and Reflection {#6.-feedback,-security-and-reflection}

## 6.1 Summary of Peer Feedback {#6.1-summary-of-peer-feedback}

Feedback was received closer to the end of the project in order to improve the user interface primarily. Most of the feedback said the mechanical quality of the game was quite high, however the graphics were very simplistic and could be improved, as it was largely black and white. With this feedback, more effort was put into ensuring that the colour design of the project was visually appealing.

While feedback has had an impact on the final product, more could have been acquired along the way to ensure that the program was performing under expectations and didn’t contain usability issues. Many bugs in the integration of the system could have been noticed and fixed earlier if peers reviewed the program while it was being produced. This would have increased the quality of the final product overall, or increased the speed in which it reached the current standard.

## 6.2 Secure Software Design and Data Handling {#6.2-secure-software-design-and-data-handling}

This project has attempted to uphold some data handling principles by minimising collected data, however fails at many other requirements. This program features very little encryption, exclusively being the hashing of the password, and simply stores information in an unprotected text file. There is some input validation, however, that merely stops the user from entering an empty username or password, though the login details are not used for any other purpose, meaning they can’t be exploited using SQL injection, as SQL is not used, nor can it be exploited using other special characters, like backslashes or question marks. There was no additional secure coding practices undertaken during development, meaning that this is poor as an example of a secure application, however, as it has no external connections, including a gitignore of the login details file, there is basically no way for a data breach to occur. 

## 6.3 Personal Reflection {#6.3-personal-reflection}

Over the course of the development of this project, I have gained some new skills and improved my ability with some others. I am more capable when dealing with various methods of external storage and different file types, and I have improved my skills when dealing with rendering using pygame. I encountered several challenges during the design and creation process, the most important to the final product being the use of dictionary to represent the position of tiles to improve performance by reducing the amount of calls to a class function, and the overall need to optimise the program, as that was a major issue in my last project.
